1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

11	# The rational-curve isomorphism `C ≅ ℙ¹` (RR.4)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.4** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma pinned in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity:290`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through the four sub-build chapters:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)`, the degree
22	  map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`, and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and Riemann–Roch in genus zero.
25	- `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`): the line bundle `𝒪_C([P])` of a closed point and
26	  the existence of a non-constant rational function
27	  `f ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P])) ∖ k̄ · 1`.
28	- **`RR.4` (this file)**: the global classification — the linear-system
29	  morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` produced from `(1, f)` (Pin 1), its degree identified
30	  via the pole divisor (Pin 2), and the degree-`1` ⇒ isomorphism step
31	  (Pin 3). The headline target `AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1`
32	  (Pin 4) is the existing pinned declaration at
33	  `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290` — see §0 below.
34

36

262	Hartshorne II §6 + IV §2 opening: for a non-constant morphism
263	`φ : C ⟶ ℙ¹` from a smooth proper curve, the function-field extension
264	`k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite, and the degree
265	`[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] = deg(φ)` is recovered as the degree of the pullback
266	of any closed point of `ℙ¹` to `C`. Specialising to `Q = ∞`, this
267	identifies `deg(φ)` with the degree of the **pole divisor** of the

277	of the headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` and does not require `g(C) = 0`).

299	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree under

336	/-- **Degree of a non-constant morphism to `ℙ¹` via its pole divisor**
337	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree
338	under finite pullback).
339	
340	For a non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
341	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically closed
342	field `k̄`, there exists a positive integer `d` (= `deg(φ)`) together
343	with a Weil divisor `D` on `C` (= the pole divisor `φ^*[∞]` of the
344	pulled-back affine coordinate) of total degree `d`.
345	
346	The non-constancy hypothesis is encoded as `∀ Q, φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q`
347	(no constant morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` agrees with `φ`); this is equivalent to
348	`φ` being surjective onto `ℙ¹_{k̄}` since the image is a closed
349	irreducible subset of `ℙ¹_{k̄}` of dimension ≥ 1, and the only such is
350	`ℙ¹_{k̄}` itself.
351	
352	iter-178+ body: a non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves
353	over `k̄` is automatically finite (proper + quasi-finite). The
354	function-field extension `k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite of some degree
355	`d = [K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] ≥ 1`. Take `D := φ^*[∞] ∈ Div(C)` via the standard
356	"pullback of a Weil divisor along a finite morphism" operation; by
357	Hartshorne II.6.9, `deg(D) = deg(φ) · deg([∞]) = d · 1 = d`. The
358	positivity `d ≥ 1` follows from non-constancy (else `deg(φ) = 0` would
359	force `φ` constant). Both the finite-pullback operation and the
360	multiplicativity-of-degree identity are project-bespoke sub-lemmas
361	threaded inside the iter-178+ body (cf. the closely related

426	lift the function-field iso to a scheme iso. -/

428	/-- **Degree-`1` non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is an
429	isomorphism** (Hartshorne I Corollary 6.12 specialised to degree `1`,
430	or equivalently Stacks tag `0AVX`).
431	
432	For smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves `C, C'` over an
433	algebraically closed field `k̄` and a non-constant morphism
434	`φ : C ⟶ C'` such that the induced `k̄`-algebra map on function fields
435	`φ^# : K(C') → K(C)` makes `K(C)` a one-dimensional `K(C')`-module
436	(equivalently `[K(C) : K(C')] = 1`), the morphism `φ` is an
437	isomorphism of `Over (Spec k̄)`-objects.
438	
439	**Signature refinement (iter-181 Lane I).** The degree-`1` hypothesis
440	is encoded as a typeclass binder
441	`[Algebra C'.left.functionField C.left.functionField]` together with
442	the equation `Module.finrank C'.left.functionField C.left.functionField = 1`.
443	The intended `Algebra` instance at every call site is the canonical
444	function-field map induced by `φ` (the composite of
445	`Scheme.Hom.stalkMap` at the generic point with the `IsFractionRing`
446	extension to the fraction-field; see Mathlib
447	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.functionField`). The previous signature took
448	an abstract existence wrapper
449	`Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)` which is strictly
450	weaker than what the iter-182+ body needs: the birational-extension
451	argument requires the iso to be **induced by `φ` itself**, not an
452	arbitrary ring iso. See `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` (Decision 2,
453	DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY) for the analysis prompting this refinement.
454	
455	iter-182+ body: invoke the scheme-theoretic argument of Hartshorne's
456	Corollary I.6.12 (Stacks `0AVX`) — equivalently
457	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.instIsIsoToNormalizationOfIsIntegralHom`
458	(see `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Steps 1–4):
459	
460	1. Reduce to `φ` finite (proper + quasi-finite ⟹ finite via
461	   `IsFinite.iff_isProper_and_isAffineHom`; quasi-finiteness follows
462	   from the degree-`1` hypothesis collapsing each fibre to a single
463	   point at the generic point and then extending by closedness).
464	2. Apply `Scheme.Hom.instIsIsoToNormalizationOfIsIntegralHom` to get
465	   `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization φ : C ⟶ normalization φ` is an iso.
466	3. Identify `normalization φ = C'` via smoothness of `C'` (smooth
467	   proper curve over `k̄` ⟹ regular ⟹ normal). The integral
468	   closure `O_{C'} ↪ φ_* O_C` is then an equality under the
469	   `finrank = 1` hypothesis (rank-`1` torsion-free coherent inclusion
470	   on a Dedekind base is an iso).
471	4. Compose to extract `C ≅ C'` in the slice category.
472	
473	Alternative route (cleaner conceptually but more sheaf API): the
474	pushforward `φ_* O_C` is a coherent `O_{C'}`-module of generic
475	rank `[K(C) : K(C')] = 1`; the inclusion `O_{C'} ↪ φ_* O_C` is an
476	iso of coherent rank-`1` sheaves (torsion-free coherent of generic
477	rank `0` is zero on a Dedekind base), so `φ_* O_C = O_{C'}` and `φ`
478	is the structure morphism of an iso.
479	
480	Blueprint reference: `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` (Hartshorne I §6
481	Corollary 6.12 p. 45 + IV §2 opening p. 299; Stacks tag `0AVX`). -/
482	theorem iso_of_degree_one
483	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
484	    {C C' : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
485	    [IsProper C.hom] [IsProper C'.hom]
486	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C'.hom]
487	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C'.hom]
488	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsIntegral C'.left]
489	    (φ : C ⟶ C')
490	    (_hφ_non_const : ∀ Q : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ C',
491	        φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q)
492	    [Algebra C'.left.functionField C.left.functionField]
493	    (_hφ_deg :
494	      Module.finrank C'.left.functionField C.left.functionField = 1) :
495	    Nonempty (C ≅ C') :=

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	\leanok
83	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
84	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
85	  
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
88	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
89	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
90	  \[
91	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
92	  \]
93	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
94	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
95	  \[
96	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
97	  \]
98	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
99	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
100	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
101	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
102	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
103	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
105	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
106	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
107	\end{lemma}
108	\begin{proof}
109	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
110	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
111	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
112	  under the name
113	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
114	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
115	  \begin{quote}
116	  \small
117	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
118	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
119	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
120	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
121	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
122	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
123	  \end{quote}
124	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
125	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
126	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
127	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
128	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
129	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
130	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
131	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
132	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
133	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
134	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
135	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
136	  project-bespoke wrapper
137	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
138	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
139	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
140	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
141	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
142	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
143	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
144	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
145	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
146	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
147	\end{proof}
148	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
149	% NOTE (iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 signature strengthening). The iter-177
150	% file-skeleton placeholder existential
151	%   "$\exists (d : \mathbb N) (D : C.\mathrm{WeilDivisor}),\
152	%      0 < d \wedge D.\mathrm{degree} = d$"
153	% was vacuous in \(\varphi\) (no constraint tying \(D\) to \(\varphi\) or the
154	% point \(\infty\)). The iter-182 plan-phase refactor (refactor slug
155	% `pin2-sig-strengthen`) replaces it with the substantive existential
156	% pinning \(D\) to \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) and the degree to the function-field
157	% extension degree \([K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\). The new typed-sorry def
158	% `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (in
159	% `RationalCurveIso.lean`, analogous to the iter-181 typed-sorry def
160	% `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` of OCofP.lean) carries the
161	% Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\); its body is queued for iter-183+
162	% via the affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route.
163	\begin{lemma}
164	\leanok
165	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
166	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  
168	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
169	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
170	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
171	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
172	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
173	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
174	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
175	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
176	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
177	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
178	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
179	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
180	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
181	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
182	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
183	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
184	  \[
185	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
186	    \quad\text{and}\quad
187	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
188	  \]
189	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
190	  \[
191	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
192	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
193	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
194	  \]
195	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
196	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
197	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
198	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
199	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
200	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
201	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
202	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
203	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
204	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
205	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
206	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
207	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
208	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
209	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
210	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
211	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
212	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
213	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
214	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
215	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
216	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
217	\end{lemma}
218	\begin{proof}
219	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
220	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
221	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
222	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
223	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
224	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
225	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
226	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
227	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
228	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
229	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
230	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
231	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
232	  is finite.
233	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
234	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
235	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
236	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
237	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
238	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
239	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
240	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
241	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
242	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
243	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
244	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
245	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
246	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
247	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
248	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
249	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
250	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
251	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
252	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
253	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
254	\end{proof}

268	\begin{lemma}
269	\leanok
270	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
271	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
272	  
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
274	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
275	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
276	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
277	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
278	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
279	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
281	  % equivalent:}
282	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
283	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
284	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
285	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
286	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
287	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
288	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
289	  (p.~299).}
290	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
291	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
292	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
293	  degree one,
294	  \[
295	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
296	  \]
297	  formally
298	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
299	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
300	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
301	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
302	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
303	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
304	  \[
305	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
306	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
307	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
308	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
309	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
310	  \]
311	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
312	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
313	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
314	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
315	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
316	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
317	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
318	\end{lemma}
319	\begin{proof}
320	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
321	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
322	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
323	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
324	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
325	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
326	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
327	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
328	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
329	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
330	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
331	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
332	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
333	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
334	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
335	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
336	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
337	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
338	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
339	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
340	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
341	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
342	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
343	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
344	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
345	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
346	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
347	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
348	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
349	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
350	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
351	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
352	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
353	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
354	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
355	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
356	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
357	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
358	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
359	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
360	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
361	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
362	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
363	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
364	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
365	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
366	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
367	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
368	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
369	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
370	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
371	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
372	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
373	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
374	\end{proof}
375	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
376	\begin{theorem}
377	
378	\leanok
379	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
380	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
381	  
382	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
383	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
384	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
385	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
386	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
387	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
388	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
389	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
390	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
391	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
392	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
393	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
394	  \[
395	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
396	  \]
397	  Concretely, the Lean signature
398	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
399	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
400	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
401	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
402	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
403	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
404	\end{theorem}
405	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
406	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
407	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
408	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
409	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
410	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
411	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
412	\begin{proof}
413	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
414	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
415	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
416	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
417	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
418	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
419	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
420	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
421	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
422	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
423	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
424	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
425	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
426	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
427	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
428	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
429	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
430	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
431	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
432	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
433	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
434	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
435	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
436	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
437	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
438	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
439	  exists a unique morphism
440	  \[
441	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
442	  \]
443	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
444	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
445	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
446	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
447	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
448	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
449	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
450	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
451	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
452	  \[
453	    \deg(\varphi)
454	    \;=\;
455	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
456	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
457	  \]
458	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
459	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
460	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
461	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
462	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
463	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
464	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
465	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
466	  and
467	  \[
468	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
469	  \]
470	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
471	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
472	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
473	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
474	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
475	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
476	   C.functionField]} instance). By
477	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
478	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
479	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
480	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
481	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
482	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
483	  closing the headline target
484	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
485	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
486	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
487	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
488	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
489	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
490	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
491	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
492	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
493	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
494	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
495	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
496	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
497	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
498	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
499	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
500	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
501	\end{proof}
502	\begin{remark}
503	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
504	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}

506	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
507	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
508	  (\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
509	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr}, body landed iter-166), whose
510	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the

512	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
513	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
514	  separately under the genus-\(0\) Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
515	\end{remark}
516	\section{Out of scope}
517	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
518	are sequenced elsewhere:
519	\begin{itemize}
520	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
521	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
522	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
523	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
524	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
525	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
526	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
527	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
528	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
529	    consumed as a black box by
530	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
531	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
532	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
533	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)

539	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
540	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
541	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
542	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
543	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
544	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
545	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean

549	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.

551	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
552	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
553	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
554	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter

46	### Decision 1: degree of a morphism between schemes

119	  has no scheme-level WeilDivisor to pull back.
120	
121	- **Cost of divergence**: zero now; non-zero once the project starts
122	  building. The natural body uses `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on
123	  each affine chart (pick an affine open `Spec A ⊂ C'` covering `∞`,
124	  then the preimage `Spec B ⊂ C` is finite over `Spec A` with
125	  `A → B` Dedekind-of-Dedekind, and `Σ_{Q over P} e(Q|P) · f(Q|P) = d`).
126	  This is `~50-100 LOC` glue around the existing Mathlib lemma.
127	
128	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (with `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` as
129	  the body-engine for iter-178+).
130	
131	### Decision 3: multiplicativity of degree
132	
133	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`
134	  (`sum_ramification_inertia`, `ramificationIdx_mul_inertiaDeg_of_isLocalRing`)
135	  is the algebraic statement. There is no scheme-level wrapper.
136	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (consume the ring-level statement inside
137	  the body).
138	
139	## Recommendation
140	
141	Both the **signature** and the eventual **body** for Pin 2 are
142	project-bespoke — Mathlib has the algebraic atom
143	(`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` in
144	`Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`) but no scheme-level
145	divisor / morphism-degree wrapper to align with. The iter-178+ planner
146	should:
147	
148	1. Keep the current signature of `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` (it
149	   is the right substantive content given the project-bespoke
150	   `Scheme.WeilDivisor`).
151	2. Plan to introduce a private `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback` (or a
152	   one-shot pole-divisor construction `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` for
153	   `φ : C ⟶ ℙ¹`) inside `RationalCurveIso.lean` or
154	   `WeilDivisor.lean`.
155	3. Reduce the multiplicativity-of-degree identity through
156	   `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on a chosen affine cover (the
157	   only point of contact with Mathlib). Budget: ~80-150 LOC for the
158	   pullback + the per-chart Dedekind-domain bridge.
159	
160	No Mathlib **upstream PR** is required — this is genuine new
161	infrastructure that Mathlib lacks, not a re-implementation. If the
162	project ever exports a `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback`-style API, it
163	*would* be worth a future Mathlib PR (Stacks tags 02RW, 02RX, 0AX5
164	cover this material), but that is out of scope for the iter-181/182
165	plan.

168	
169	For the eventual upstream PR (post-project), the following would close
170	the gap in Mathlib:
171	
172	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.degree : (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIntegral X]
173	  [IsIntegral Y] [IsFinite f] [LocallyOfFiniteType f] : ℕ`
174	  defined as `Module.finrank Y.functionField X.functionField`.
175	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor` (currently project-bespoke).
176	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsFinite f] :
177	  Y.WeilDivisor →+ X.WeilDivisor`.
178	- `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_pullback` (Hartshorne II.6.9):
179	  `(f^*D).degree = f.degree * D.degree`.
180	
181	The project must not block on any of these — the in-tree route is the
182	correct call for iter-181/182.

62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
138	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
139	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
140	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
141	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
142	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
143	
144	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
145	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
146	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
147	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
149	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
150	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
151	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
152	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
153	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
154	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
155	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
156	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)

161	
162	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
163	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
164	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
165	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
166	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
167	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
168	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
169	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/

171	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
172	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
173	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
174	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
175	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
176	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
177	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
178	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
179	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
180	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
181	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
182	  instance below.) -/
183	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
184	
185	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
186	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
187	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
188	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
189	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
190	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
191	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
192	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
193	
194	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
195	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
196	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
197	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
199	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
201	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
202	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
203	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
205	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
206	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
207	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
208	
209	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
210	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
211	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness

226	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
227	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
228	    (f : X.functionField) :

237	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
238	
239	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
240	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
241	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
242	
243	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
244	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
245	
246	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
247	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
248	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
249	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
250	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
253	
254	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
255	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
256	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
257	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
258	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
259	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
260	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
261	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
262	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
263	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
264	hypothesised regime. -/
265	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
266	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
267	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
268	
269	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
270	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
271	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
272	multiplicity one. -/
273	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
274	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
275	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
276	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
277	
278	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
279	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
280	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
281	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
282	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
283	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
284	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
285	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
